CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Mr. M. O’'Dea, of Stafford Street, a member of a
well-known Dublin firm, in the course of a letter con-
cerning Irish furniture, says:— My firm have tried
all they knew to have cheap middle-class furniture
made in Dublin, and we were at all times prepared to
pay 10 to 12 per cent. extra for the Dublin-made
article as a set-off agaiust carriage and breakages on the
imported goods; yet, I regret to say, we have not suc-

ceeded. The Dublin Industrial Association are using

every legitimate effort to find work and wages for the
Dublin tradesmen.  What are those men doing for
themselves, or for Irish manufacture? Do they buy
only Irish boots, clothing or furniture? Are they help-
ng the Irish employer to produce cheaply, and keep

ut the foreign goods?” 3

usiy enough, in the same day’s paper in' which
he letter from which the above extract is taken, there
;ﬂso a letter from Mr. W. P. Pantridge, a working-

n and one of the recognised leaders of workingmen

in this country. Portion of Mr, Partridge’s letter an-
swers Mr. O’Dea’s question. He writes:—‘In this
matter (the purchasing of goods made in Ireland by
Irish labour) the Irish workingman sins—and sins
blindly. He regards the amounts he purchases to be
so small as to be unworthy of the thought whether the
goods bought be made in Ireland or not.”

‘We are sorry to hear from one who is so competent
to speak on the matter that the Irish workingman is
at once so unpatriotic and so stupid. However, we
cannot but applaud the frankness of Mr. Partridge.
We have ourselves touched on this matter over and over
again. We remember referring to the patriotic Dublin
workman who rocks his baby to sleep in a foreign-made
cradle though one as good and as cheap could be pro-
cured of Irish make. The ‘‘consumer’’ in many minds

* becomes a sort of abstraction which all can “afford to kick,

and people are prone to forget that we all—manufac-
turers, middlemen, editors, and artisans—are consumers.
If, as Mr. Partridge states, Irish workingmen don’t
bother whether their purchases were made in Ireland or
made out of it, why should the Irish workingman expect
others to bother whether their building materials, their
printing, their locomotive engines, and so forth, are Irish
or not? We are for Irish goods for Irish people on
business principles, but we are for it all round; and if
Irish workingmen don’t care a jack straw where their
boots are made the recollection of that fact does not
feed our national indignation when we hear that some-
body has imported his slates or his furniture.

We have before us a letter of which the following is
a copy : —“ 28 Clare Street, Dublin, 28th March, 1906.
Dear Sir,—Pending the appointment of a secretary, I
have been asked to send out notices for the first meeting
of the committee on the unemployed appointed at Lein- _
ster House on 23rd inst. The meeting will take place
at 5 p.m. on Tuesday, 3rd April, at 188 Great Bruns-
wick Street, to consider the preliminary arrangements,
co-option of new members, .and other matters.  The
Lord Mayor will preside.—Yours truly, P. Hanson.”
Now on turning to the light. the paper upon which this
“ committee on the unemployed ’’ letter is written we
find the words “Crown Vellum,” a lion, or something
that purports to be a lion, on top of a crown, and on
the other side something which strikes our untutored
eyes as ‘‘the lion and the unicorn fighting for the
crown, the lion beat the unicorn upside down.”” Why
did not Mr. P. Hanson, of 28 Clare Street, write upon
paper made in Ireland

The Kilanerin (Co. Wexford) branch of the United
Irish League has taken sides with temperance reform.
Why should not every branch of the United Irish
League go and do likewise? Of course we know many
reasons why they do not, and probably will not do so.
One is that in many cases their patriotism is
not, if we may wuse the term in this con-
nection, bona fide. It is easy if fruitless work
passing resolutions calling upon the British Govern-
ment to do this, that, and the other; suppose for a
change all the branches of the United Irish League
called upon the members of the Irish Party to support
the St. Patrick’s Day Closing Bill, and to second Mr.
William Redmond’s efforts towards local option. But .
then these resolutions might be effective! As far as
the interests of temperance legislation goes the Irish
Parliamentary Party is little or no good. While you
have young Mooney and Pat O’Brien, the beardless self-
advertiser and blocker of the St. Patrick’s Day Bill, on
one side, you have on the other poor Mr. William
O’Brien’s weekly screech adorned with a choice selection
of advertisements of Bungeries.

130

‘We learn from the London correspondent of Alf Foxz
that the Church of England arranged services on Good
Friday evening in certain London theatres and music
halls! A Rev. Mr. Webster officiated at the Oxford
Music Hall and a Reyv. Stuart Holden at the Empire!
‘When the Church of England has to go to London
music halls on a Good Friday evening it looks bad for
Christianity in “Saved ’’ England. What is the Church
of England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland doing? Are
they content to lag behind their English sister? Why
did’ they not hold services in Dan Lowry’s and the
popular little house on Burgh Quay,’’ and so catch the
“Saved ”’ importations and others who take more kindly
to music halls than to churches. If the Irish branch,
or rather the branch in Ireland, of the Church of Eng-
land should decide to follow the lead of the Protestant
Bishop of London’s Evangelical Council, arrangements
might be made for the services of a select few of the
‘“ Souper ’’ ministers.

At a demonstration of the British Independent Labour
Party Mr. Keir Hardy, M.P., declared: —“ A day was
coming when the people should, indeed, be free—free
from poverty, free from wealth, free from politicians,
free from superstition, free from self-deception—should
stand erect, free men and free women. They looked
to an age of fraternity, brotherhood, and fellowship.”
We wonder, Mr. Hardy, is the day coming when the
people will be free from raimeis? Scarcely, so long as
you are on the stump.

What is the value of a resolution? In November
last, at the Newcastle West Board of Guardians and
District Council, the following resolution, proposed by
Mr. T. J. Keane, and seconded by Mr. E. Sweeny, was
passed unanimously :— That in future all professional,
clerical and official positions in the gift of this Council
be given only to those candidates who pass, previous to
the date of election for such positions, a prescribed test
in the Irish language, provided such candidates be
otherwise duly qualified; and that in the case of all
other positions in the gift of the Council preference be
given to candidates having a knowledge of Irish, pro-
vided they are otherwise suitable.”” That was in No-
vember, when probably the horizon was perfectly clear.

In due time there was, as it were, a cry of a sail, a
sail, and there was excitement on shore: a six and
eightpenny barque hove in sight, or, in other words,
the post of solicitor to the board became vacant. The
son of the previous solicitor was rushed into thé job
with extraordinary rapidity. At the meeting that the
father’s resignation was announced the son was sho-
velled in. There was no advertising, no competition.
‘What of the resolution of November 30th? But then,
perhaps, Irish “resolutions’’ are like pie-crusts, only
made to be broken. The eloquent Guardian who pro-
posed the resolution of November spoke strongly in
favour of the appointment a few months afterwards.
The Knockaderry Branch of the Gaelic League has al-
ready, at a special meeting, condemned *the unprin-
cipled and inconsistent action ’’ of the Newcastle West
Board of Guardians. Probably the Newcastle West
branch will take the matter up as well as other neigh-
bouring branches. After all, when a public body passes
a language resolution it ought to stick by it or else be
called upon to explain the reason why.

The smoking concert of the ‘‘ Irish’’ branch of the
Railway Benevolent Institution was held recently under
the patronage of the chairman and directors of the four
chief Railway Companies in this country. The per-
formers at the concert were, like Napoleon Dent, im-
ported or dumped into Ireland; they called themselves
the ““ smart set.”” Needless to say there was not a song
in Irish on the programme—of course, Brother Gould-
.ing, of Kilho’s, is too much a ‘‘ gentleman ’’ to allow
such vulgarity. One of the performers sang a ‘‘coon’’
lyric by the name of ‘“ Go away, Mr. Crocodile.”” We
are sure that is just the sort of thing that Napoleon:
Dent would declare is ‘ beastly jolly, don’t-che-know.’’
Another performer sang ‘ The mighty deep.”  The

Rathmines is now, or ought to be, a happy place.
The ratepayers are privileged to pay 10s. 6d. in @he _£,
and have the honour of being governed by the chlldhk,e,z
and bland old Mr. Edmundson and his “saved
friends. If there were five instead of two wards, the
ratepayers might suffer in two ways—there might be
more low-down “Idolators’” in the Council, and the
rates might be very much lower! And how could Rz_tth;
mines of the bogus lodgers stand that state of affairs?
Still money is money all the world round; and when
rates go up strange things happen. Here we have old
Mr. Edmundson quite cross with Alf Fox on the head
of some anonymous letter that appeared in the hb?l!ler
of Canon McInerney. And Mr. Bolton said that It
was hard lines that such statements should be made
anonymously in a newspaper, and that the only chance
of answering them was a newspaper controversy, which
he, for one, had not the slightest intention of en'termg
upon.”” How concerned they are about ‘‘ hard lines.”
Is it not hard lines that the unfortunate ratepayer of
this district should have to pay an extra 1s. 9d. in the
£7 A Mr. Hewson sought to have the rates lowered.
It was easy, he said, to adopt a heroic policy when the
money came out of other people’s pockets. Quite so.
This Mr. Hewson also remarked:—‘‘ People did not
realise the increase, and persons he had come across
really did not know on earth what had happened to the
Township. It was a disgrace, an everlasting blot on
the escutcheon, as the Chairman and Mr. Fawcett
would be ruined in reputation for life.”” That’s rather
hard on old Mr. Edmundson, the childlike and bland,
for Mr. Hewson is not one of the three ‘‘ Idolators’’
who have béen graciously admitted to the Council via
the two-ward tickets. Mr. Hewson is “saved ’’ and is
one of England’s Faithful Garrison.

What about presenting, a testimonial to Mr. Edmund-
son in recognition of the able manner in which he pre-
sided over the Rathmines Council that is now demand-
ing a trifle of 1s. 9d. in the £ extra from the ratepayers!
Or, if a testimonial is too much, why not a complimen-
tary dinner? Duly certified bogus lodgers could be let
in for half-price, and the dinner could be held on a
Friday, so that the ‘“saved ’’ would have all the meat
for themselves, just as they have nearly all the fat jobs
in the gift of the Council.

We have received several circulars, etc., concerning
the Munster-Connacht Exhibition that is to be opened
during July next. Needless to say, we are very much
in sympathy with the enterprise and with the interests
it is founded to promote. In this abnormal country
there are some abnormal circumstances attending the
forthcoming Exhibition. The Chairman and Vice-
President is the Right Worshipful the Mayor of Lime-
rick, Alderman Joyce, M.P. Amongst the members of
the committee we counted no less than thirty-eight
Nationalist Members of Parliament, including Mr. Jno
Redmond, Mr. Wm. O’Brien, and Mr. Dillon. The
Exhibition promoted by, amongst others, thirty-eight
Nationalist M.P.’s is to be opened by the Lord Lieu-
tenant of Ireland and the Countess of Aberdeen! It is
indeed an abnormal country., The Lord Licutenant is
the representative of Edward VII., yet thirty-eight
Nationalist M.P.’s, including Mr. John Redmond, Mr.
John Dillon, and Mr. William O’Brien, are on the

committee of this Exhibition which is to be opened by

the Lord Lieutenant and his wife. Dunraven, of
‘‘ Devolution ”’ fame, is President of the Munster-
Connacht Exhibition, of which Mr. John Dillon is one
of the mere committee—another abnormality !

' If the English Education Bill passcs, when may we
- expect the British Nonconformist conscience ”’ to
pounce upon the schools of Ireland? Even the Trish
Catholic Intermediate schools receive help out of the
public funds in the way of Exhibitions, etc., and the
Nonconformist ‘¢ conscience *’ may ask why should St.
Colman’s, Fermoy, the convents of St. Louis and the
other distinguished educational establishments for
Catholic boys and girls receive any help out of the
public funds so long as they teach Catholicity as part
of the school work! We think it is none too soon that
" the ‘“Idolators’’ of this country should look to their
defences. In the meantime the ‘ Idolators’’ would do
well to rally to the side of the Catholic Defence Society.

The current number of our popular monthly contem-
porary The C.¥Y.M. is up to its usual standard. The

Whilst we are doing something to interest our readers
in the great question, the national question, of Irish
Insurance for the Irish people it is well that such in:
surance companies as we have should not be overlooked.
For instance, we have the Irish Vehicle Owners’ Acci-
dent Co., Ltd., in Exchequer Street, Dublin, which
claims that it is the only Irish office that confines itself
solely to third party Indemnity Insurance. No doubt
it only deals with a chip from the great Irish Insurance
log, but in contemplating the larger problem the exist-
ence of this Irish company should not be overlooked.
Tt stands on its merits, and competes with all comers;
and therefore it is reasonable to ask why send money
out of the country if the particular class of risk in
which it deals can be taken up in Ireland.

Yet another peeler testimonial. Ballina is not a
centre of a too prosperous country, yet they ‘raised
the wind > down there for one Mr. Monson, a ‘‘ saved ’’
policeman. Mr. Monson is a District Inspector, and
““on his departure from Ballina’’ a certain coterie of
busybodies, including the inevitable Mr. Bung, could
not remain easy unless they inflicted a ‘‘ hat ’’ on their
neighbours in favour of the policeman who has received
orders to ‘“move on.”” The subscriptions are strictly
limited to a guinea a head. Major Ormsby, Mr. J. B.
Dillon, J.P.; Mr. John Garvey, Crown Solicitor, are
amongst those concerned in this latest infliction on Bal-
lina. Mr. John Flanagan, an ‘‘ Idolator,”’ is treasurer,
and Mr. M. V. Coolican, also an ‘‘ Idolator,’”’ is hon.
sec. The printed touting circular before us bears the
water-mark ‘‘ Large Post,”” and we never heard of that
water-mark in connection with Irish-made paper. But
then the elité of Ballina may be too ‘‘classy’’ to use Irish
paper ; nation-making projects in the shape of sending
round the hat for a policeman is probably more in keep-
ing with the respectability of the Jay Pees and such
like aristocrats of the neighbourhood.

Co., writes: —
. Recently a friend of mine handed me a copy of your
journal, wherein I see the question of starting an Irish
Insurance Company in Ireland is advocated.
Last summer during my visit to Ireland I had an
opportunity of gauging the business outlook throughout
the country, and I then thought as I do now, that with

afforded for the formation of a purely Irish organisation
of this kind. I note that this’is the fourth of a series
of articles on the subject in your journal, and am not
therefore familiar with the present sbnge_of the contro-
versy, as I have not seen the former articles. 'I glean,
however, from the present article that tl{e writer, who -
is evidently an insurance man of consxdera_,ble expe-

ROYAL UNIVERSITY—ELECTION OF A
SENATOR.
UDGE SHAW having resigned his position as
Senator of the Royal University, the vacancy will
be filled up at the meeting of Convocation to be held
at 4 p.m. on Friday next, 20th April, in the University
Building, Earlsfort Terrace. ~Dr. Edward Magennis,
M.D., of 37 Harcourt Street, Dublin, is one of three
candidates whose addresses are now in the hands of the
members of Convocation. He has been nominated by
twenty-two of his fellow-members ; and, as a well-known
medical gentleman, a distinguished graduate, and a
sound patriot, no better choice could be made. We
strongly recommend him to the electors.

The second candidate, Dr. John Campbell, of Belfast,
dwells prominently on the fact that he is “ strenuously
opposed to the doctrinal element in education.”” ‘I
shall withstand,”” Dr. Campbell goes on to say, ‘‘any at-
tempt to establish a sectarian University or a sectarian
College in this country.”” In plain language this means
that Episcopalians having got the ‘“sectarian ’’ College
known as Trinity, and Presbyterians having got the
“ sectarian’’ Belfast Queen’s College, any attempt to
get a College which Catholics can safely attend will be
bitterly opposed.

It will be noted, too, that Dr. Campbell limits his
opposition to proposed colleges ““ in ¢his country >’ (Ire-
land). He does not object to Oxford and Cambridge,
which from his point of view are strictly ‘‘ sectarian’’ ;
nor to the Presbyterian Colleges in Scotland.  Dr.
Campbell’s statement, indicates that he will oppose any
settlement likely to give moderate satisfaction to
Catholics; and, no doubt, he will, in this, have the
support of the most intolerant class in Europe—Belfast
bigots.

Mr. James Perry, C.E., is County Surveyor in Gal-
way, and, as an Engineer, he points out that the Senate
is already well supplied with Theological, Legal and
Medical experts. Mr. Perry says—‘‘ My personal
opinion is that the common public funds should nof be
expended directly in the teaching of Dogmatic Theo-
logy.”” Perhaps, this is a rap at Trinity—at all events,

Catholics, when they ask for funds in aid of a College
which they, as Catholics, can safely attend, unever
dream of asking for funds for the teaching of Dogmatic
Theology. In a somewhat halting way Mr. Perry says:
“ T have lived for nearly thirty years in Connaught, and
T have no cause to complain of any abnormal want of
religious toleration on the part of my neighbours.””

Let us put the question plainly to County Surveyor
Perry: During the thirty years he has been in Con-

naught, has he had any cause to complain of want of
religious toleration on the part of the Catholics, who
form 97 per cent. of the whole population? If so, what
abnormal *’ or

@

was the cause of complaint—whether

“normal”’? In such matters it is better to speak
plain! an to insinuate. :
Pla\’?‘;eb;gal}ll watch the proceedings on to-morrow (Friday)
with interest. If the Catholic candidate (Dr.
Magennis) be beaten, the fault will lie chiefly with the
hundreds of Catholic Graduates who have neglected to
have themselves registered as members of Convocation.
Of those who are registered we expect a large attend-
ance, and those who cannot attend personally should be
careful to send in voting papers. g

THE DUBLIN MUSICAL SEASON.

ITH the third concert of the Orchestral Society
the present musical season may be looked upon
as closed, so far as Dublin is concerned. Stray odds
and ends of concerts may turn up, but the organic
season itself is ended. Let us have a look over it, and
indulge in a few reflections. I think the event of the
season was the starting of the Sunday Afternoon Con-
certs at the Antient Concert Rooms. Here for the first
time in Ireland was tried the experiment of introducing
the highest class of orchestral music at popular prices,
and it is not only interesting, but really important to
consider the result. But why so important? For this
reason: folk-art is dead in Ireland, and it is important
to study the attitude of the people towards high or
developed art. At once it may be said that the abti-
tude of the Sunday audiences was, on the whole, dis-
tinctly favourable. But did these audiences really re-
present the people? That is a question far less easy to
answer. On the whole, I think the audiences were re-
presentative of our all too scanty middle class. Doubt-
less there were also listeners from the classes above and
below the middle, but, so far as I could judge, I think
the broad general populace was not much represented
there. True enough, the cheaper seats were always
most occupied, but the occupants as a rule had not a
pronouncedly democratic air about them. Of the men,
a large part seemed made up of clerks and students who
mildly affect culture—and I am not saying ‘‘ affect ’’
in any cynical sense; the women were largely of the
type one thinks “ musical.”” In fact, to a great extent,
the patrons of these concerts seemed to be the sort of
people one expects to find there. The audiences con-
tained a goodly sprinkling of veterans—of those old
regulars who turn up whenever the programme has
Beethoven or some other notable name upon it, and
who want to hear a good class of music at any price.
I fancy, for instance, that many of the people in the
sixpenny seats would have paid a shilling had that been
the lowest price, and many of those in the shilling seats
would have paid two shillings, had that been the lowest
price for the same accommodation.

Of the concerts themselves nothing but what is good
can be said. The programmes were excellent; far
more attractively made up, as a rule, than those of
a similar class in London, while an even higher standard
of taste was adhered to. No weak concessions were
made to merely “ popular ’ taste; yet the pieces given
were almost invariably received with marked favour.
To be sure, the popular leanings peeped out sometimes,
and one could see that though Beethoven was received
with good countenance, certain bits of showy—albeit
artistic and attractive—virtuosity were greeted with
real rapture.  One thought of ‘the fable of the cat
turned into a young lady. Mademoiselle behaved ad-
mirably at table until she spied a mouse on the floor;
then she sprang up and gave chase with a most unlady-
like eagerness, proving that “a cat will after kind,”’ as
Touchstone says to Orlando. A flute solo of . Mr.
H. Leeming’s, and a ’cello solo of Mr. Clyde
Twelvetrees—it was Popper’s piquant “ Spanish Dance’’
—swayed the hearts—and hands—of the audience much
more strongly than could Beethoven or Schubert. How-
ever, the pieces were very nice, and the audience had
every right to applaud the melodious Popper when they
liked him so well. I cannot forget that they received

. the andante of Beethoven’s Fifth Symphony with a

degree of favour beyond what I was prepared for,

while Handel’s noble “Largo’’ seemed to rouse
genuine feeling. The orchestral piece that fell in for
the warmest and heartiest welcome was Brahms’s (set-
ting of)  Hungarian Dances.”” For once Signor Es-
posito consented to give a repeat. One thing disap-
pointed me: the reception given to the dances from
Tschaikowsky’s “Casse-Noisette "’ Suite. I did not
mind their being a bit puzzled at the “ Chinese’’ dance,
or the “Danse Arabe,”’ but I thought nothing could
stand up before the matchless ¢ Trepak’ (Russian
dance). But they received it just like the others, with
scanty, uncertain applause. I could not understand
it; I had thought the “ Trepak ' would set anyone on
fire, but on this occasion it was damply received—be-
yond believing! Perhaps the highly novel orchestration
of these dances had something to do with the bepuzzled
air of their reception, for the Valse des Fleurs, with
which the suite concludes, is orchestrated in a more
usual style than its predecessors, and was given a more
gracious reception. However, on the whole, I am well
satisfied of one thing: that high-class musical art has
no terrors for these audiences, and quite evidently does
not spell Boredom in capital letters to them. For the
playing of the orchestra itself no praise could be much
too high; but then the performers are all professionals.
This being so, and the conductor being a musician of
Signor Esposito’s ability and taste, one need not wonder
at the results achieved. What one does wonder at is
that there should not have been packed houses every
time. Why is it that people will rush to hear some
Blue—or Green, or Purple—Hungarian Band, or some
quasi-charlatan like Creatori, while they come lan-
guidly, if at all, to an essentially better band, playing
a far higher order of music? But there is little use in
asking such questions. The main point is that the con-
certs did not, I understand, pay for themselves; all the
greater is the honour due to those on whom the deficit
falls, and who are paying it. The concerts are to be
resumed next season we are told, when it is to be
hoped they will receive the support their high merits
deserve.

The concerts of the Dublin Orchestral Society, like
those in the Sunday series, reflect honour on Signor
Esposito, who has given Dublin a lead in the matter
of concerted instrumental music which it much needed.
These concerts, too, are not so rosy a financial success
as could be wished; the greater is the pity. The
Society has now given, with full artistic success, some
of the largest works of even the advanced modern
masters; the playing of Tschaikowsky’s masterpiece at
the last concert of the season was a fine proof of what
the band can now do. By the way, the rendering of
the lovely music which Signor Esposito wrote for Dr.
Hyde’s “The Tinker and the Fairy ”’ was a treat. On
the who}e, one has nothing to wish but that the Orches-
tral Society could make as big a success financially as it
does musically.

Other musical events and enterprises do not call for
prolonge@ notice. Signor Bozzelli’s choral combination
did not lift its head above the waters this year. This
is rather a pity. His one performance last year of
Handel’s “ Belshazzar,’’ though not excellent all round
was at least tolerable as regards the choruses, and
served to give one an idea of a fine but neglected work.
Will the Bozzelli Society rise no more tobthe surface?
Mr. Barton McGuckin’s Society seems to Pproceed upon
the notion that Dublin is panting for an amateur ver-
sion of two of those well-worn operas which professional
combinations are wont to make their bread and butter
by. I predict that a very moderate quantum of much-
sung opera, rendered by amateurs, will prove to be as
much. as Dublin really wants—if it really wants any.

choir, the bulk of the soloists being sadly below the
mark. Taken all round, I think the honours of the
season rest indisputably with Signor Esposito and his
band. IMAAL.

THE MEANING OF THE ENGLISH EDUCATION
ACT.

(1) All National Schools shall henceforward be Model
Schools, and ‘‘ simple Bible teaching ’’ may, if the
County Councils desire, be given at the public ex-
pense in all such schools.

(2) In National Schools, which were hitherto under
clerical management, the County Council may, if
they desire, permit religious teaching to be given
twice a week on the same terms as hitherto, except
that it shall not be given by the teachers of the
schools. 3

(3) A fair rent shall be paid by the County Council for
the use of schools hitherto under clerical manage-
ment.

(4) All teachers in all schools shall be appointed by the
County Councils, without regard to their religious
views.

(5) No child shall be compelled to be present at any
religious teaching. g

(6) Arrangements are made making it difficult for the
trustees of clerical schools to run them as other
than Model Schools.

This, as I understand it, is how the New English
Education Bill, apart from the special exception to be
mentioned hereafter, would read if we can suppose its
provisions applied to our own country. There may, no
doubt, be some inaccuracy in this method of explaining
it, as, for instance, in the difference in the religious
composition of the Irish County Councils,” but yet 1
think it is the only way in which to bring its compli-
cated provisions home to Irishmen. ; :

Tt will be seen at once that, from any outsider’s point
of view, the measure is one of extraordinary injustice
and want of logic. It brings us back to the date of the
fifty years’ war by re-enacting the principle ubi regio,
ibi religio, since it actually establishes one form of re-
ligious teaching, namely, ‘simple Bible teaching,”
which means the highest common factor of Christian
beliefs. It thus in a way goes once again through the
-same process that was gone through at the Reforma,.bion.
The English Government was then face to face with a
‘body of Calvinists on the one hand and a body of people
who cherished Catholic, doctrine and practices on the
.other. The Church of England was the result. A sort
.of compromise was made compulsory on all, and as be-
tween Nonconformists and members of the Church of
England, a similar method, equally illogical and equally
“unjust, is now being adopted. Since Nonconformists

_object to payment for the teaching of the Church of

“England and other religions out of public money,
and neither Churchmen nor Nonconformists favour
ar?”” or “godless’” teaching, a weak - form
jous teaching, more in accord with Non-
y than the Established Church, but not

frustrate the object of the amendment.

THE LEADER. 135

divectly in conflict even with the latter, is being
taken up as a State religion and endowed out
of public funds. Thus there will arise the extraordi-
nary spectacle of Jews paying for teaching elementary
Christianity without receiving denominational endow-
ment themselves, and Catholics, similarly, providing an:
endowment for the singing of Protestant hymns.

Mr. Birrell, in his explanation of the Bill, admitted
this injustice, but he claimed that, as between Noncon-
formist and Churchman, it was a fair Bill, and indeed
it is not impossible that, having regard to the number
of low churchmen, broad churchmen, and indifferentists
among the Church of England laiety, like various
other illogical institutions of England, it will, as be-
tween Churchmen and Nonconformists, work well enough,
though the Church of England clergy seem determined to
fight it. The new Bill is not then in any way an endow-
ment of ‘“Paganism’’ or ‘‘secularism,’’ as some may have
expected. It is, on the contrary, that very much older
device the establishment (in education) of a State re-
ligion which shall be taught at the public expense, may
be taught in all schools, is the only one permitted to be
taught in many schools, and, in any case, the only one
to be paid for out of public moneys in any school what-
ever.

So far, however, I have left out of account the special
provision, whatever its value, made for the benefit of
Jews and Catholics and intended to lighten the injustice
of what is admittedly a Protestant settlement. The
Bill also provides, n effect, that in ex-Jewish and ex-
Catholic and some other schools the County Council
may give facilities for the teaching of the Jewish and
Catholic religion (but not at the public expense) during
school hours and by the teachers of the school, which
last is not permitted in ex-Church of England schools.
If such facilities are given there will be no ‘‘simple
Bible teaching’’ in the school. This provision will also
apply to some Church of England schools. So far then
the Catholic position may be summed up thus: —

Negative—(1) They lose all control over the school
and all right to appoint teachers.

(2) They will have to pay for their re-
ligious teaching themselves, presum-
ably out of the rent paid by the Co.
Council for use of the school buildings.

(38) Even so, it is optional with the Co.
Council whether they will permit this
teaching or not.

(4) The teachers in their schools may be
Turks, atheists, etc., and will very
probably not be Irishmen or Catholics.

(5) Though Catholics receive no money,
save rent, for their own religious
teaching, they will have to pay for
the ‘“simple Bible teaching’’ of the
other schools.

Positive—(1) The Catholic religion may be taught
during school hours, if the Co. Coun-

N a recent speech on Insurance, Mr. Sexton, with
I reference to the disappearance as Irish institutions
of the National and Patriotic, expressed himself to the
effect that in any country in the civilised world with
which Ireland could be compared, local capital and
local enterprise would promptly come forward to fill
such vacancies. He might have gone much funther.

. He might have hazarded the assertion that in no other

country would all sections of the population have ex-
hibited for eighty years and more such marked pre-
ference for insurance offices managed by foreigners in-
stead of supporting their own existing offices; or that

- local enterprise and capital, if willing to act at all,

would postpone all action whatever until the last flicker
of life had departed from the sole survivor in their
midst. Nor can I think that in either of the countries
named by Mr. Sexton would the banking establishments
all over them be in the least disposed to represent

foreign insurance offices to the exclusion of native ones,
or be allowed to do so were they ever so much inclined,

It will be urged that England and Scotland are not
foreign countries, and that with Ireland they are part
and parcel of the same political unit. And, of course,
this is so. ‘But do English bank managers represent
Scotch insurance offices to the exclusion of English ones,
or the Scotch bank managers English offices to the ex-
clusion of the Scotch? Everybody should be aware that
the exact contrary is the fact. And here a thought in-
trudes itself for which expression may be excused:
Why should bank managers represent insurance officés at
all? In other words, why should a struggling shop-
keeper on obtaining an advance of money to enable
him to keep his shop open, have to pay ten or twenty
pounds for a life policy for which he has no need, and
which he, of course, forfeits as soon as he releases him-
self from the bank’s clutches, or becomes insolvent? In
some districts in Scotland (and doubtless the same thing
is true of Ireland) the pest became so intolerable that
the directors of one of the leading banks had the matter
under their “serious consideration ;’’ nothing, however,
(appropriately enough) resulted from this Campbell-
Bannerman-like attitude, and so the nuisance con-
tinues in its pristine vigour in all three countries. That
the great bulk of Irish Protestants have always looked
upon England and Scotland much as a Yorkshireman
regards Lancashire or Middlesex is an undoubted fact;
and, of course, accounts, in a large measure, for the
inability of the National and Patriotic to have ever done
more than the merest fraction of their country’s in-
surance business, seeing that the trade and commerce
of Ireland—for so long a period during the last cen-
tury—continued to remain almost exclusively in Protes-
tant hands; although seeing that the National and
Patriotic were Protestant institutions in fact if not in
name, greater recognition of them by the Protestants
might with confidence have been expected.

The feeling amongst Protestants (not, however, alto-
gether confined to them) that it is immaterial whether
an insurance office has its headquarters in Dublin, or
Belfast, or in London, or Edinburgh, goes far to explain
the otherwise inexplicable absence of insurance institu-
tions from so great a commercial and maufacturing cen-
tre as the city of Belfast. It will hardly be disputed that
if Belfast had been situated in any portion of Great Bri-
tain—or in Canada or Australia—it would long sinece
have been in possession of half a dozen wealthy offices.
transacting every description of insurance business not
merely throughout Ireland, but in Great Britain and
the Colonies—if not also in the United States. For
Irish Catholics to insist upon it so often that supine-
ness and enertia are qualities they possess exclusively is
to act with very great unfairness towards their Protes-
tant fellow-countrymen.

The task, then, of founding an insurance office in Ire-
land as a purely commercial venture, and appealing in-
differently to all sections of the population, seems—
‘things being as they are—to be an undertaking only
describable as foredoomed; unless, indeed, it is sup-
ported and representative either of a religious denomi-
nation or of some powerful trade influence. Nor need
we go very far back in our experience in search of
forcible illustrations.

Six or seven years ago it being determined by a num-
ber of Dublin traders to found a fire insurance company,
a board of directors was formed, half Catholic and half
Protestant; the necessary capital was subscribed, or
what was thought to be such; premises were taken, and
the Irish Commercial Insurance Company, Limited, in-
vited business from all sections of the public, apnd all
parts of the country. What was the result? A gpeedy
collapse, mot as a consequence of conflagrations,
but through sheer inability to obtain bnsiness.
The Irish public would not insure with the new Irish
office, so it came to an end. It was literally still-born.
. A few years after this unfortunate Dublin office closed
its doors, those of another one were opened, But in-
stead of appealing for support to all denomipations it
confined itself to one only, and to the clerical portion
only even of that. Its success was as immediate, andin
every respect as substantial, as in all simila.fly consti-

of between three and four thousand pounds—not a shil-
ling of which can be diverted to any other insurance

sentences quite Addisonian—and the IFreeman in its

way, in which connection it would be interesting to know
what proportion the gross premiums of his company bear
to the met; or, in other words, to what extent Mr.
Jorkins’s company is an Irish insurance office—and to

The majority of the Law Loxds, in the case of the
Free Church of Scotland, have decided that no religious
opinion or tenet is to be admitted as a ground for
transferring trust property, except it is proved by evi-
dence. This decision they have grounded on Lord
Eldon’s decision in Craigdallie v. Aikman. It is, then,
a settled fundamental principle of Chancery Law. This
principle has been most flagrantly violated and set at
nought by the Governors of the Erasmus Smith schools.
For they have taken away the Endowment from the
tenants’ children and others designated by Erasmus
Smith, and given it to others who have none of the
qualifications prescribed by Erasmus Smith on the plea
of alleged Protestant intentions in Erasmus Smith, of
which intentions they have not, on their own showing
and admission, a particle of evidence. Lord Justice
Fitzgibbon and his friends have violated the intention
of Erasmus Smith which they have admitted, namely,
that of giving free education to his tenants’ children,
on the plea of having regard to the spirit of a Protes-
tant intention, of which they have no evidence what-
ever!!!

The law affecting the Erasmus Smith Endowment is
most clear and distinct, admits of no doubt, but infal-
libly secures the Endowment for Erasmus Smith’s
tenants, the tenants of his Corporation, and for 20
other poor children within two miles of each of his
three Free Schools. Tudor, p. 136, states the law in
the most unmistakable manner. He says:—“ Where
the object of the testator’s bounty is clearly defined,
and his charitable purpose is capable of being carried
into effect, and is not contrary to law, his directions
must be strictly followed. In such a case there is no
power to apply the gift to any other purpose than that
which he has marked out. The mere fact that the gift
might be more usefully applied in some other way, is
no ground for departing from the intention of the
donor.” * These words exactly describe, and apply to,
the Erasmus Smith Endowment. For (a) “the object
of “ Erasmus Smith’s bounty is clearly defined.”” The
persons for whose benefit his bounty is to be applied are
most clearly defined and designated; they are the
tenants and inhabitants on the clearly defined areas of
his estates in Ireland, and of the estates of his cor-
poration, and of the districts extending two miles
around each of his three Free Schools at Drogheda,
Galway, and Tipperary. The precise form which the
bounty is to take has been defined with equal clearness.
It is to give free secular education—Primary, Inter-
mediate, University and Technical, together with cloth-
ing to poor children in his schools.

The object of his bounty (b) “is capable of being car-
ried into effect.”” For the Endowment may be applied
to the free secular education of his tenants’ children, of
the children of the tenants of his Corporation, and of
the poor children within two miles of each of his Free
Schools.

The object of his bounty (¢) ¢“is not contrary to law.”
For the Penal Laws have been repealed; and no law
now forbids the appointment of Catholic trustees, or
Catholic schoolmasters, or Catholic instruction in the
schools. Therefore, in the case of the Erasmus Smith
Endowment (4) “there is no power to apply the gift
to any other purpose than that which Erasmus Smith
has marked out.”” And even if the Erasmus Smith’s
gift (¢) “ were more usefully employed ”’ in Trinity
College, in Blue Coat Hospital, in Harcount Street
School, in Brunswick Street School, and in the English
Schools, . than in educating his tenants ’children, that
“would be no ground for departing from intention.’’

Now, so far as the proof of the trust of the Erasmus
Smith Endowment is concerned, I need not write a word
more. I have made out a case in fact and law upon
which any judge should declare the trust to be for the
free secular education of the children of Erasmus
Smith’s tenants, of the children of the tenants of his
Corporation, and of the “other poor children’’ desig-
nated by him. But I shall give other ‘proofs of the
Trust, and refute other false arguments- of my

~ _The Jaw as laid down by Tudor, p. 136, and given
above is confirmed by the decision of the Lords in the
cage of the Free Church of Scotland.

The Lords have decided that the Trust established
and precisely defined by the founder, must be carried
into effect, where it is possible to do so; and that, con-
sequently, neither the trustees, nor the beneficiaries,
nor the Courts have any power to alter such Trusts
where it is possible to carry them into effect. They
have also decided that the basis of the Trust precisely
defined by the founder, and precisely determining who
are the beneficiaries, cannot be altered either by the
trustees, or the beneficiaries, or the Courts.

In two respects the case of the Erasmus Smith En-
dowment and the case of the Free Church of Scotland
are alike (1) The basis of the Trust determining who
are the beneficiaries was freely chosen by the donor, or
donors, in each case. (2) The basis of the Trust was
clearly defined by the donor, or donors, in each case.
Hence, the decision of the Lords has confirmed the Trust
for the free secular education of the temants’ children
in the Erasmus Smith case.

The two cases differ as to what formed the basis of
the Trust in each case. In the case of the Free Church
of Scotland, the basis of the Trust was a collection of
religious doctrines. In the case of the Erasmus Smith
Endowment, the basis of Trust is the relation of tenancy
with Erasmus Smith and of occupancy upon all his
Irish estates, the relation of tenancy with his Corpora-
tion, and of occupancy upon all their estates, and the
relation of occupancy on the districts reaching two miles
around each of his three Free Schools. Through ignor-
ance of this distinction the Editor of the Irish T'imes
quoted the decision of the Lords of August, 1904, on the
Free Church, as a confirmation of Lord Justice Fitz-
gibbon’s judgment on the Erasmus Smith Endowment.
The decision of the Lords did the very opposite.

It declared Lord Justice Fitzgibbon’s judgment
and the action of the Erasmus Smith Board
to be illegal. The Lords merely decided that

neither could the trustees, now the beneficiaries, nor
the Courts alter the basis of the Trust determining who
are the beneficiaries. The basis of Trust in the Free
Church of Scotland was a collection of religious doc-
trines. And the majority of the Law Lords, five
against two, decided that the Trustees of the United
Free Church had changed that basis, or, collection of
doctrines, and had thereby lost all right to the Trust
funds. The basis of Trust in the Erasmus Smith En-
dowment is not religion—religion, as I have proved,
does not form any part of it—and hence the decision
of the Lords does not fix any religion for it—it is, as I
have shown, the relation of tenancy and occupancy on
the estates of Erasmus Smith, and of his Corporation,
and of occupancy of a specified district round each of
his three Free Schools. These relations determine pre-
cisely and individually who are the beneficiaries of the
Erasmus Smith Endowment; and the Lords have de-
cided that the Endowment must be given to them and

- to no others. And the basis of Trust in the Erasmus

Smith Endowment is fixed and immovable; it is the
same to-day as it was in the days of Erasmus Smith,
and will be the same as long as grass will grow or water
run.

. Although the body of doctrine which formed the
basis of the Trust in the Free Church was, as I have
said, clearly defined, yet two of the Law Lords differed
from the other five as to what it was. But there can
be no difference of opinion among judges, or among any
others, as to what is the basis of Trust in the Erasmus
Smith Endowment. For the estates and schools are
therein more fixedly defined than the Atlantic. In the
words of Mr. Justice O’Brien :—* The lands are there,

There is another point of difference between the Free
Church case and that of the Erasmus Smith Endow-
ment. In. the Free Church case the doctrines which
formed the basis of the Trust were freely chosen by the
founders. The religious conditions imposed on the
Erasmus Smith Endowment were imposed, not by Eras-
mus Smith, but by Cromwell and Charles II. T}Je de-

Lord Justice Fitzgibbon has told us, Rep. 1894, pp.
236 and 238, that the paramount intention of Erasmus
Smith was to propagate the Protestant, faith. Erasmus

ticing to trades, and also to give clothing to his tenants’

shall publiquely in the Schoole or Church every Lord’s

the first Lord’s day in July, or within a month, if noe
lawfull impediment doe hinder in full assembly in the

By “the Intent and use of the said schools’’ in this
passage is meant the paramount intention of Erasmus
Smith. That it is the paramount intention is, also,
proved by the fact that Erasmus Smith has commanded
his schoolmasters to publish it: once a year  under pain

In this quotation, after Erasmus Smith had directed
that the catechism should be taught every Lord’s day
in the school or church to the scholars, and to others
who should be present, he immediately commands that
another and a different, publication should be made once
a year, in order to make better known the Intent and
use of his schools. Thus, after he had directed that
the propagation of Protestantism should be published
on every Lord’s day, 52 times a year, he tells us that
another and a different publication is necessary, in order
that “the inhabitants neighbouring to the schools’’
should the better know the Zatent and use of the schools
or his paramount intention. Now, if the Infent and
use of his schools, or his paramount intention, had been
the propagation of Protestantism, there would be no
need of making another publication once a year, in
order to make it known after it had been pub].isheé 52
times a year. Therefore, the propagation of Protes-
tantism was not his paramount intention.

. CORRESPONDENCE.

CoRK INDUSTRIAL ASSOCIATION

Cork, 9th April, 1906.
Dear Sir—I notice in local papers to-day that at a
meeting of above association great surprise was expressed
at the negligence of subscribers in sending in their an-
nual subscriptions, and also at the failure of the appeal
for a 5,000 shilling fund.

The writer has recently met persons who, like himself,
have no recollection of seeing a balance sheet of the as-
sociation’s ever since it was started, and as there is an
erroneous impression owing to the great success of the
Exhibition that a large balance is on hands, T think
it would be well if some particulars were published.

T would also like to know who elects the Council, and
when does the election take place?—Truly yours,
